questions.questionText: A 50-year-old presents with flaccid blisters that rupture easily on normal-appearing skin. Nikolsky sign is positive. Biopsy shows intra-epidermal (suprabasal) acantholysis. Direct immunofluorescence shows intercellular IgG in a 'fishnet' pattern. Diagnosis?
questions.optionB: Pemphigus vulgaris (autoimmune intra-epidermal blistering — anti-desmoglein 3/1 antibodies, flaccid blisters, positive Nikolsky, suprabasal separation)
questions.explanation: Core concept: PV = intraepidermal, flaccid, Nikolsky+, fishnet IgG, anti-desmoglein. Why correct: PV: anti-desmoglein 3 (mucosal) and anti-desmoglein 1 (cutaneous). Intercellular IgG in fishnet/honeycomb pattern on DIF. Suprabasal acantholysis. Row of 'tombstone' basal cells. Why A wrong: Bullous pemphigoid: SUBepidermal, tense blisters, Nikolsky NEGATIVE, anti-BP180/BP230 (hemidesmosomal), LINEAR IgG + C3 at BMZ. Why C wrong: DH: granular IgA at dermal papillae tips, associated with celiac disease (anti-tTG IgA). Why D wrong: Linear IgA — linear IgA at BMZ (not intercellular). Clinical relevance: PV: mucosal (oral) involvement in ~90% — often first presentation. Potentially fatal (sepsis). Treatment: systemic corticosteroids + steroid-sparing agents (rituximab now first-line in some guidelines). Exam trap: Pemphigus = IntraEpidermal = Flaccid = Fishnet IgG; Pemphigoid = SubEpidermal = Tense = Linear IgG. Memory aid: 'PemphigUS = SUPERficial (intraepidermal); pemphigOID = deep (subepidermal).' Reference: IADVL Textbook – Vesicobullous.
questions.conceptTag: Pemphigus vulgaris intraepidermal Nikolsky desmoglein fishnet

questions.optionB: Celiac disease (gluten-sensitive enteropathy — granular IgA deposits at dermal papillae tips on DIF, responds to dapsone and gluten-free diet)
questions.explanation: Core concept: DH = skin manifestation of celiac disease. Why correct: DH: intensely pruritic, grouped (herpetiform) vesicles on extensor surfaces (elbows, knees, buttocks). DIF: granular IgA at dermal papillae. Anti-tTG IgA positive. >90% have subclinical celiac disease. Why A wrong: Hep B associated with PAN, mixed cryoglobulinemia. Why C wrong: SLE — butterfly rash, discoid lupus, not DH. Why D wrong: HIV — many dermatologic conditions but not specifically DH. Clinical relevance: Treatment: dapsone (rapid symptomatic relief) + gluten-free diet (treats underlying cause — diet alone can clear skin but takes months). Complications of dapsone: methemoglobinemia, hemolytic anemia (G6PD deficiency). Exam trap: IgA granular at papillae tips = DH. IgA LINEAR at BMZ = Linear IgA dermatosis. Deposition pattern differs. Memory aid: 'DH = Dapsone + gluteN-free diet + granular IgA + celiac.' Reference: IADVL Textbook – Vesicobullous.
questions.conceptTag: DH celiac IgA granular dapsone gluten
